content:
Ryan, that’s an honest question, and I feel the weight of it.

A few guiding thoughts:

- God is honored not by our desire to look respectable, but by our humble submission to what He actually said (2 Tim 3:16; Gal 1:10). At the same time, He doesn’t ask us to carry burdens He didn’t place on us (Acts 15). So our task is to read Scripture as it means to be read—according to its genre, language, and authorial intent—not to win cultural approval or to be deliberately contrarian.

- “Literal” doesn’t mean “wooden.” Inerrancy means the Bible is true in all it affirms, in the way it intends to affirm it. We all speak of “sunrise” without teaching geocentrism; that’s ordinary, phenomenological language. Scripture often speaks that way too.

- On the “firmament” (raqia): faithful, conservative scholars who love the authority of Scripture differ. The Hebrew word can mean something “spread out” and Genesis uses it for the place where birds fly and where God sets the lights—what we’d call the sky/heavens. Some argue it implies a solid dome; others see ordinary sky/expanse and the “waters above” as clouds or the cosmic waters pictured in ancient imagery. The point of Genesis 1 is crystal clear—God alone created, ordered, and rules everything; the sun, moon, and stars are His creatures, not gods—while the precise physical mechanics are not its purpose. Because the text itself allows some range here, binding consciences to one technical reading as a test of fidelity is unwise.

- About social cost: following Jesus will cost us something (1 Cor 1:18). We shouldn’t edit Scripture to avoid embarrassment. But we also shouldn’t make the path harder by insisting on conclusions Scripture doesn’t demand (Rom 14). Let the only offense be the gospel and the clear teaching of God’s Word, not our extra requirements.

So, is God “big enough to be honored either way”? He’s honored when we:
1) submit to His Word as our final authority,
2) handle it carefully and honestly,
3) refuse to fear man,
4) show charity where the text leaves room.

If you are convinced the text teaches a particular view of the firmament, hold it with integrity and humility. If you’re not convinced the text requires a “solid dome,” you can fully honor Scripture and embrace mainstream science without compromising inerrancy. Either way, don’t make this the hill that eclipses the hills Scripture itself makes central: God the Creator, the goodness of His world, humanity in His image, sin, redemption in Christ, and the hope of new creation.

Pastorally: teach your kids that we don’t chase respectability, and we also don’t confuse boldness with needless offensiveness. We aim to “adorn the doctrine of God” (Titus 2:10) with truth, clarity, and grace. If you want, we can look at the key texts and word studies on raqia so you can see the options Scripture itself allows.
